perturbation.original_text: 5. Maintenance of Confidentiality. Each party agrees that it shall take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information of the other party. Without limiting the foregoing, each party shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own most highly confidential information and shall ensure that its employees and agents who have access to Confidential Information of the other party are subject to confidentiality obligations similar in scope and nature to the provisions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees or agents. Each party shall reproduce the other party’s proprietary rights notices on any such approved copies, in the same manner in which such notices were set forth in or on the original.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Maintenance of Confidentiality. Each party agrees that it shall take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information of the other party. Without limiting the foregoing, each party shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own information and shall ensure that its employees and agents who have access to Confidential Information of the other party are subject to confidentiality obligations similar in scope and nature to the provisions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees or agents. Each party shall reproduce the other party’s proprietary rights notices on any such approved copies, in the same manner in which such notices were set forth in or on the original.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'reasonable' and 'most highly confidential' from section 5, there is a contradiction with section 2. Section 2 defines what confidential information is, so by removing 'reasonable' from Section 5, the party does not need to be reasonable when it comes to protecting the information, the party needs to take steps to protect the information. By removing 'most highly confidential', it changes the meaning in protecting the confidentiality. Before, you had to protect it at the highest level, now it is not at the highest level. This creates uncertainty and a contradiction as it is unclear what level of confidential information is.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. “Confidential Information ” means any information (including without limitation documents, computer data, or oral communications) disclosed by either party to the other party, either directly or indirectly, that is identified as confidential or other similar designation at the time of disclosure or is known or should reasonably be known by the Recipient to be confidential in nature. The existence and subject matter of the parties’ discussions shall be treated as Confidential Information. Confidential Information may also include information disclosed to Owner by third parties. Confidential Information shall not, however, include any information which (i) was publicly known and made generally available in the public domain prior to the time of disclosure by Owner; (ii) becomes publicly known and made generally available after disclosure by Owner to Recipient through no action or inaction of Recipient; (iii) is already in the possession of Recipient at the time of disclosure by Owner as shown by Recipient’s files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (iv) is obtained by Recipient from a third party without a breach of such third party’s obligations of confidentiality; or (v) is independently developed by Recipient without use of or reference to Owner’s Confidential Information, as shown by documents and other competent evidence in Recipient’s possession.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 5 required 'reasonable measures' to protect 'most highly confidential information'. The changed text removes both 'reasonable' and 'most highly confidential', altering the standard of care. Section 2 defines 'Confidential Information' and implies a reasonable understanding of what constitutes confidential information. By removing 'reasonable measures', the revised Section 5 now contradicts the implicit understanding of reasonableness established in Section 2's definition. Furthermore, removing 'most highly confidential' weakens the protection requirement, potentially conflicting with the expectation that information identified as confidential should be protected accordingly as defined in Section 2. Therefore, there is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Return of Materials. All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and remain the property of Owner and shall be promptly returned to Owner upon Owner’s written request or upon termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Return of Materials. All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and remain the property of Owner and shall be promptly returned to Owner upon Owner’s written request or upon termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Confidential' from 'Confidential Information', the definition of 'Information' now contradicts the original intent in Section 2. Section 2 defines 'Confidential Information'. Section 8 now suggests all information, not just confidential information, needs to be returned which is a contradiction. All information is a broader set and creates conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. “Confidential Information ” means any information (including without limitation documents, computer data, or oral communications) disclosed by either party to the other party, either directly or indirectly, that is identified as confidential or other similar designation at the time of disclosure or is known or should reasonably be known by the Recipient to be confidential in nature. The existence and subject matter of the parties’ discussions shall be treated as Confidential Information. Confidential Information may also include information disclosed to Owner by third parties. Confidential Information shall not, however, include any information which (i) was publicly known and made generally available in the public domain prior to the time of disclosure by Owner; (ii) becomes publicly known and made generally available after disclosure by Owner to Recipient through no action or inaction of Recipient; (iii) is already in the possession of Recipient at the time of disclosure by Owner as shown by Recipient’s files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (iv) is obtained by Recipient from a third party without a breach of such third party’s obligations of confidentiality; or (v) is independently developed by Recipient without use of or reference to Owner’s Confidential Information, as shown by documents and other competent evidence in Recipient’s possession.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the word 'Confidential' from 'Confidential Information' in Section 8. This creates a contradiction with Section 2, which specifically defines 'Confidential Information'. Now, Section 8 implies that all 'Information', regardless of whether it is confidential or not, must be returned, which contradicts the defined scope of what was originally meant to be returned, leading to confusion and potential disputes over what information is subject to the return obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Term. This Agreement covers the disclosure of all Confidential Information for a period of three (3) years commencing as of the Effective Date. Recipient’s duty to protect the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement expires five (5) years from the date of receipt of Confidential Information (the “ Confidentiality Term ”). Either party may terminate this Agreement earlier by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. Upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, the obligations of each party shall survive with respect to Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder until such time as the respective Confidential Information becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of Recipient or until the end of the Confidentiality Term, whichever occurs sooner. For the avoidance of doubt, upon termination of this Agreement, each party’s obligation to keep the Confidential Information of the other party confidential for the Confidentiality Term shall apply even in the event where one party is acquired or merged by or into a third party, and such third party shall have the right to enforce this obligation as a third party beneficiary.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Term. This Agreement covers the disclosure of all Confidential Information for a period of three (3) years commencing as of the Effective Date. Recipient’s duty to protect the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement expires five (5) years from the date of receipt of Confidential Information (the “ Confidentiality Term ”). Either party may terminate this Agreement earlier by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. Upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, the obligations of each party shall survive with respect to Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder until such time as the respective Confidential Information becomes publicly known and made generally available. For the avoidance of doubt, upon termination of this Agreement, each party’s obligation to keep the Confidential Information of the other party confidential for the Confidentiality Term shall apply even in the event where one party is acquired or merged by or into a third party, and such third party shall have the right to enforce this obligation as a third party beneficiary.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or until the end of the Confidentiality Term, whichever occurs sooner' from section 10, it creates a contradiction with the earlier part of Section 10. Specifically, it mentions that Recipient's duty expires five years from the date of receipt. Removing the later text now makes the confidential agreement run for unlimited time, which contradicts itself within the section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Term. This Agreement covers the disclosure of all Confidential Information for a period of three (3) years commencing as of the Effective Date. Recipient’s duty to protect the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement expires five (5) years from the date of receipt of Confidential Information (the “ Confidentiality Term ”). Either party may terminate this Agreement earlier by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. Upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, the obligations of each party shall survive with respect to Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder until such time as the respective Confidential Information becomes publicly known and made generally available.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the confidentiality term expires either when the information becomes public or at the end of the five-year Confidentiality Term, whichever occurs sooner. The changed text removes 'or until the end of the Confidentiality Term, whichever occurs sooner.' This creates a contradiction because Section 10 also states, "Recipient’s duty to protect the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement expires five (5) years from the date of receipt of Confidential Information." Removing the phrase makes the survival obligation potentially indefinite if the information does not become publicly known, contradicting the explicit five-year expiry. This contradiction aligns with the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction, as essential language was removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated elsewhere in the same section. The removal creates an inconsistency within section 10 of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Remedies. Each party agrees and acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to the other party for which monetary damages may be inadequate. Accordingly, the harmed party may be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Remedies. Each party agrees and acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to the other party. Accordingly, the harmed party may be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement by the other party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'for which monetary damages may be inadequate', it creates a contradiction with the intent. It implies monetary damage would be adequate, but you are still seeking injuctive releif. This could allow a party to state monetary is more appropriate than injuctive.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Remedies. Each party agrees and acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to the other party. Accordingly, the harmed party may be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement by the other party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that monetary damages 'may be inadequate', justifying the pursuit of injunctive or equitable relief. The changed text removes this justification, implicitly suggesting that monetary damages might be adequate. This creates a contradiction because, without the original justification, seeking injunctive relief could be challenged, conflicting with the contradicted section, which allows for injunctive relief.
